skill.name: azure-cosmos-ts
skill.description: Guides TypeScript and JavaScript developers using the @azure/cosmos SDK for Azure Cosmos DB data-plane work, including authentication, document CRUD, queries, bulk operations, partition keys, and container access patterns.
skill.summary: Build Azure Cosmos DB data-plane workflows with the @azure/cosmos TypeScript and JavaScript SDK.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backtick patterns, but review shows they are Markdown code fences and inline package names, not executable Ruby or shell behavior. The skill is documentation for Azure Cosmos DB SDK usage and includes legitimate environment-variable credential examples, so publication is acceptable with a low-risk note about handling database credentials and destructive operations carefully.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as External Commands
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Markdown fenced code blocks and inline SDK examples. They are not executable skill files, command wrappers, Ruby backticks, or hidden shell automation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review confirms the flagged backticks delimit documentation examples. No separate script or executable command path was found in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Environment Variables Used for Cosmos DB Configuration
low_findings.description: The examples read Cosmos DB endpoint, key, connection string, database, and container names from process.env. This is normal SDK configuration, but copied examples can expose or misuse credentials if users log values or store secrets insecurely.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The process.env usage is explicit and semantically tied to Azure Cosmos DB connection settings. The skill recommends AAD over keys, which lowers the concern.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Placeholder Network URL
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is a placeholder Cosmos DB endpoint in an environment variable example. It does not send data to a third-party endpoint or define an unauthorized network request.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL uses an account placeholder and appears only in an environment setup block. No exfiltration endpoint or automatic request is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Heuristics
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography hit on the frontmatter description and reconnaissance hit on a partition-key best practice do not correspond to dangerous code. The only cryptographic API shown is crypto.randomUUID in a service example, which is appropriate for generating item identifiers.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA-1, insecure cipher, or reconnaissance command at the cited locations. The cited content is metadata and database performance guidance.

low_findings.title: Destructive Database Operations Included as Examples
low_findings.description: The skill documents delete, replace, patch, upsert, and bulk operations against Cosmos DB containers. These are legitimate capabilities, but users should review target databases and partition keys before applying generated code to production data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly perform normal data-plane mutations, including deletion and bulk writes. This is intended behavior for the skill, not malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Database Credential Configuration Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes process.env examples for Cosmos DB endpoint, key, and connection string. Treat these as sensitive application secrets when adapting the examples.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly reference COSMOS_KEY and COSMOS_CONNECTION_STRING. The pattern is normal for SDK setup but needs secure secret management.

content.user_title: Build Azure Cosmos DB TypeScript Workflows
content.value_statement: Cosmos DB data-plane work often needs correct authentication, partition keys, and query patterns. This skill gives focused @azure/cosmos guidance for safe TypeScript and JavaScript implementation.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install @azure/cosmos and @azure/identity for Node.js projects.
Explains AAD and key-based CosmosClient authentication patterns.
Provides examples for create, read, replace, upsert, patch, and delete operations.
Demonstrates SQL queries, parameterized queries, pagination, and cross-partition queries.
Covers bulk operations, hierarchical partition keys, ETags, and common ErrorResponse handling.
Includes a service layer pattern for wrapping container access in TypeScript.
limitations: It focuses on the Cosmos DB NoSQL data-plane SDK, not Azure Resource Manager provisioning.
It does not create or validate Azure credentials for the user.
Examples need project-specific database, container, and partition key values.
It does not replace production review for cost, indexing, consistency, and data retention decisions.

use_cases.title: Add Cosmos DB Access to a Node Service
use_cases.description: Use the skill to wire CosmosClient authentication and container access into an existing TypeScript service.

use_cases.title: Review Query and Partition Key Choices
use_cases.description: Use the examples to compare parameterized queries, pagination, and partition key access patterns before implementation.

use_cases.title: Implement Bulk Data Workflows
use_cases.description: Use the bulk operation guidance to design batched creates, upserts, reads, replacements, patches, and deletes.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create an Azure Cosmos DB client in TypeScript using @azure/cosmos and DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Add Document CRUD
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me implement typed create, read, replace, upsert, patch, and delete operations for a Cosmos DB container.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Query Safety
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Cosmos DB query approach and convert unsafe string queries into parameterized SqlQuerySpec patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Production Service Layer
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a TypeScript service layer for Cosmos DB with partition key handling, ETag concurrency, pagination, and 429 error handling.

output_examples.input: I need to connect a TypeScript API to Cosmos DB with Azure identity.
output_examples.output: A concise setup plan covering package installation, DefaultAzureCredential, CosmosClient construction, and required environment variables.

output_examples.input: My query filters products by category and maximum price.
output_examples.output: A recommended parameterized query structure with notes about partition keys, pagination, and avoiding string interpolation.

output_examples.input: I need to update many documents and handle failures.
output_examples.output: A bulk operation approach with per-operation status handling, retry guidance for rate limits, and warnings for delete operations.

best_practices: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential and managed identity before key-based authentication.
Use parameterized queries and include partition keys whenever possible.
Test destructive and bulk operations against non-production data first.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode Cosmos DB keys or connection strings in source files.
Do not build SQL query strings by concatenating user input.
Do not run cross-partition or bulk operations without cost and data-safety review.

faq.question: Does this skill provision Cosmos DB accounts?
faq.answer: No. It covers the @azure/cosmos data-plane SDK. Use Azure management tools or @azure/arm-cosmosdb for provisioning.

faq.question: Can it help with TypeScript types?
faq.answer: Yes. It lists common SDK types and shows typed item and query patterns.

faq.question: Does it support Azure identity authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends DefaultAzureCredential and shows key-based authentication as an alternative.

faq.question: Can it help prevent query injection?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends SqlQuerySpec parameterized queries instead of string interpolation.

faq.question: Does it include production error handling?
faq.answer: It covers common Cosmos DB errors such as 404, 409, 412, and 429. Production systems may need broader retry and observability logic.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with production data?
faq.answer: Review generated code before use. The skill includes delete and bulk write examples that can change production data.
